lesson_title: Naming collisions and an introduction to namespaces

cards.text: A {{c1::naming collision}} occurs when two {{c2::identifiers}} have the same {{c3::name}} in the same {{c4::scope}}, causing a conflict.
A {{c1::namespace}} provides a way to group related identifiers and keep them separate from other identifiers with the same name in different {{c2::namespaces}}.
The standard library is defined in the {{c1::std}} namespace. To access it, use the {{c2::scope resolution operator}} {{c3::::}}

cards.question: What does std::cout mean?
cards.explanation: std:: specifies that we're using cout from the standard (std) namespace, not some other cout that might exist.

cards.text: The statement {{c1::using namespace std;}} allows you to use identifiers from std without the {{c2::std::}} prefix.

cards.front: Why is 'using namespace std;' considered bad practice?
cards.back: It defeats the purpose of namespaces by bringing all std names into global scope, increasing the risk of naming collisions and reducing code clarity.

cards.question: What problem does this code have?
cards.code:
using namespace std;
using namespace foo; // foo also has 'cout'

cout << "Hello"; // Which cout?
cards.answer: Naming collision: Ambiguous which cout to use (std::cout or foo::cout).
cards.explanation: Using multiple namespaces can cause collisions when they have identifiers with the same name.

cards.text: The scope resolution operator {{c1::::}} tells the compiler that an identifier is inside a specific {{c2::namespace}}.
You can create {{c1::custom namespaces}} using the {{c2::namespace}} keyword to organize your code and avoid {{c3::naming conflicts}}.

cards.explanation:
Follow these guidelines:
  • Prefer explicit std:: over using namespace std
  • Never use 'using namespace' in header files
  • Use 'using' declarations sparingly for specific identifiers if needed
  • Group related code in custom namespaces
  • Keep namespace names short but descriptive

cards.back: Scope resolution operator - used to specify which namespace an identifier belongs to

cards.front: What namespace are identifiers in if no namespace is specified?
cards.back: The global namespace. Identifiers defined outside any explicit namespace are in the global namespace.
